questions.q: In which town does Leh Palace stand?
questions.explanation: Leh Palace overlooks the town of Leh, the historic capital of the Ladakh region.

questions.q: In which present-day Union Territory is Leh Palace located?
questions.explanation: Leh lies in Ladakh, which became a separate Union Territory of India in 2019.

questions.q: How many storeys tall is Leh Palace?
questions.explanation: Leh Palace is a nine-storey structure, one of the tallest buildings of its era in the region.

questions.q: In which century was Leh Palace built?
questions.explanation: Leh Palace was constructed in the 17th century, in the early 1600s.

questions.q: Which famous Tibetan building is Leh Palace said to resemble?
questions.explanation: Leh Palace is often described as a smaller model of the Potala Palace in Lhasa, Tibet.

questions.q: Leh Palace was built as a royal residence for the rulers of which kingdom?
questions.explanation: It served as the royal palace of the kings of Ladakh, the Namgyal dynasty.

questions.q: What kind of structure is Leh Palace?
questions.explanation: Leh Palace is a former royal palace of the Ladakhi kings, now a heritage monument.

questions.q: Leh Palace is built on the slope of which hill above the town?
questions.explanation: The palace sits on Tsemo hill, also called Namgyal hill, rising above old Leh.

questions.q: Which Indian government body is responsible for the conservation of Leh Palace?
questions.explanation: The Archaeological Survey of India maintains and restores Leh Palace as a protected monument.

questions.q: Leh Palace lies along which historic trade network?
questions.explanation: Leh was a key trading hub on caravan routes linking the Silk Road, with goods passing between India, Tibet, and Central Asia.

questions.q: Which Ladakhi king is credited with building Leh Palace?
questions.explanation: Leh Palace was built by King Sengge Namgyal, one of the most powerful rulers of the Namgyal dynasty.

questions.q: To which ruling dynasty did the builder of Leh Palace belong?
questions.explanation: Sengge Namgyal belonged to the Namgyal dynasty, which ruled Ladakh from the 15th to the 19th century.

questions.q: Sengge Namgyal, builder of Leh Palace, is sometimes given which epithet?
questions.explanation: Sengge means 'lion' in Tibetan, and he is often called the 'Lion King' of Ladakh.

questions.q: What are the thick walls of Leh Palace primarily made of?
questions.explanation: The palace was built using locally available stone, sun-dried mud bricks, and wood, typical of Tibetan-Himalayan architecture.

questions.q: Why did the Ladakhi royal family eventually abandon Leh Palace?
options: They were defeated and forced to leave by Dogra forces in the 19th century
questions.explanation: The royal family abandoned the palace in the 19th century after the Dogra conquest of Ladakh under Zorawar Singh.

questions.q: After leaving Leh Palace, the Ladakhi royal family relocated to a palace in which nearby village?
questions.explanation: The Namgyal family moved to Stok Palace, across the Indus, which remains associated with the family today.

questions.q: What can visitors enjoy from the upper floors and roof of Leh Palace?
options: Sweeping views of Leh town and the surrounding mountains
A view of the Taj Mahal
questions.explanation: Its hilltop position gives panoramic views over Leh and the distant Stok Kangri and Zanskar ranges.

questions.q: Which broad architectural tradition does Leh Palace belong to?
questions.explanation: Leh Palace reflects medieval Tibetan architecture, with sloping walls, flat roofs, and tiered storeys.

questions.q: What does Leh Palace house today after restoration?
options: A museum displaying artefacts, paintings, and royal objects
questions.explanation: The restored palace functions as a museum, showing thangkas, old paintings, jewellery, and ceremonial items.

questions.q: Leh Palace is overlooked from above by which older fortified structure on the hilltop?
questions.explanation: Higher up on the same ridge stand the older Tsemo Fort and the Namgyal Tsemo Gompa.

questions.q: What is the dominant religion historically reflected in Leh Palace and its surrounding monuments?
questions.explanation: Ladakh has long been a centre of Tibetan Buddhism, reflected in the palace's prayer rooms and nearby gompas.

questions.q: Approximately at what altitude does Leh, the setting of the palace, lie?
questions.explanation: Leh sits at roughly 3,500 metres above sea level, making the palace one of the highest in India.

questions.q: Around which years was Leh Palace constructed?
questions.explanation: Construction is generally dated to the early seventeenth century, around the 1600s to 1620s, during Sengge Namgyal's reign.

questions.q: Which monastery, also linked to Sengge Namgyal's patronage, became the largest in Ladakh?
questions.explanation: Sengge Namgyal was a great patron of Buddhism and is associated with Hemis Monastery, the largest gompa in Ladakh.

questions.q: Who was the father of Sengge Namgyal, the king who built Leh Palace?
questions.explanation: Sengge Namgyal was the son of King Jamyang Namgyal of the Namgyal dynasty.

questions.q: The Dogra general who conquered Ladakh and helped end royal use of Leh Palace was named?
questions.explanation: General Zorawar Singh led the Dogra conquest of Ladakh in the 1830s-1840s, ending Namgyal rule from Leh.

questions.q: Which design feature helped Leh Palace withstand the region's earthquakes and harsh climate?
questions.explanation: Like the Potala, its massive walls taper inward as they rise, giving stability against tremors and the cold desert climate.

questions.q: What was the historic role of Leh, the town below the palace, in regional commerce?
options: A major caravan trading centre between India, Tibet, and Central Asia
questions.explanation: Leh was a thriving caravan crossroads where traders exchanged pashmina, salt, grain, and goods across the Himalayas.

questions.q: Which river valley does the Leh region, home to the palace, sit within?
questions.explanation: Leh lies in the upper Indus river valley, with the Indus flowing through Ladakh near the town.

questions.q: What status does Leh Palace hold with respect to UNESCO World Heritage listing?
options: It is a UNESCO World Heritage Site
It is a protected ASI monument but not a UNESCO World Heritage Site
It was delisted by UNESCO in 2010
It is jointly listed with the Taj Mahal
questions.explanation: Leh Palace is a nationally protected monument under the ASI but has not been inscribed as a UNESCO World Heritage Site.
